topic: Dubai brunch culture and trying to eat healthy at buffets

turns.content: Can we talk about the absolute TRAP that is Dubai brunch culture?? Because I went to one of those Friday brunches yesterday and I am still recovering haha
Dubai's Friday brunch scene is legendary — and legendarily excessive. What happened? Did the buffet get the best of you?

The buffet DESTROYED me, querida. Completely destroyed me. It was at one of those big hotel brunches — you know, the kind with twelve different food stations, a sushi bar, a carving station, a dessert room that is literally a separate room just for desserts. And I walked in telling myself 'Bruna, you are a health professional, you will make good choices.' I lasted about four minutes haha!

Four minutes of good intentions — that's honestly impressive given what those hotel brunches look like. The way they're designed is almost engineered to overwhelm your decision-making. When you're facing twelve stations, the sheer variety triggers a 'try everything' response that's hard to override. What was the damage by the end?

Okay so — lobster mac and cheese to start, then lamb from the carving station, sushi (at least that was somewhat healthy??), some kind of truffle pasta that I did NOT need, a cheese board because I cannot resist cheese, and then THREE desserts. Three!! A crème brûlée, a chocolate tart, and mango sticky rice. I ate for like two and a half hours. I had to unbutton my jeans in the car. Ai meu Deus, who AM I??

That is a comprehensive tour of the buffet. The two-and-a-half-hour timeline is part of the problem too — those brunches are designed to be long, social, leisurely events, which means you're grazing continuously rather than having one defined meal. Your satiety signals don't stand a chance when the eating window stretches that long. Is this a regular thing for you?

I go maybe once a month? My friends here love brunch culture — it's like THE social activity in Dubai. Everyone does Friday brunch. And I love the social part! Sitting with friends for hours, talking, laughing — that's very Brazilian actually, we do long meals too. But in Brazil a long meal is rice and beans and maybe a beer. Here it's a 500-dirham all-you-can-eat extravaganza with seventeen types of cheese. The scale is insane.

The social aspect is genuinely valuable — long meals with friends are good for the soul, and there's a clear cultural parallel with Brazilian dining. The challenge is that the Dubai version wraps that social experience in an environment designed to maximize consumption. Have you tried any strategies for navigating it more moderately, or do you just accept it as a monthly indulgence?

I've TRIED strategies!! I tell myself — start with salad, fill up on protein and vegetables first, then if you want a small dessert, fine. And sometimes it works! But the problem is when you're sitting there for two hours and your friends are going back for round three and someone says 'oh my God you HAVE to try the beef cheek' — resistance is futile haha. The social pressure plus the visual abundance is just too much for my willpower.

Willpower is a losing strategy in that environment — the research on decision fatigue and food variety is clear that more options lead to more consumption regardless of intention. A more structural approach might help. For example, do one full survey lap before putting anything on your plate, so you can see everything and prioritize what you actually want versus what just looks good because it's there.

The survey lap!! That's actually genius. Because you're right — half of what I eat at brunch I eat just because it's THERE, not because I actually wanted it. Like did I really want truffle pasta? No! But it was in front of me so I took some. If I walked the whole buffet first I could be more strategic about what deserves plate space.

Exactly — it turns a reactive process into a deliberate one. Once you've seen everything, you can build a plate around your actual priorities rather than accumulating things station by station. You might also find that your first-lap picks look very different from what you'd grab impulsively. What would your priority plate actually look like if you were being intentional?

Honestly? Sushi and desserts haha! Those are the things that genuinely excite me. The carving station and the pasta are fine but I can get lamb and pasta anywhere. Good sushi and an elaborate dessert spread are what make brunch special. So maybe I should just own that — go straight for my favorites, enjoy them properly, and skip the filler foods I eat out of obligation to the buffet. Nossa, why did it take me this long to figure this out??

Sometimes you need someone to reflect back what you already know. If sushi and desserts are your real priorities, build around those and you'll probably enjoy the brunch more while eating less overall — because you're eating intentionally rather than reactively. And there's nothing wrong with a monthly indulgence that brings you joy and connection.

That's what I keep telling my patients too!! One meal doesn't define your health. Consistency over perfection. I'm great at giving this advice, terrible at following it myself haha. But okay — new brunch protocol: survey lap, pick my real favorites, enjoy them without guilt, and stop sampling every single station like I'm a food critic being paid for a review. I spend so much energy stressing about brunch when I should just enjoy it!

There's something ironic about a health professional stress-eating at a buffet and then stress-worrying about the buffet afterward. The stress might be worse for you than the crème brûlée. Go to brunch, enjoy your sushi and your mango sticky rice, have a great time with your friends, and let the rest go. One intentional plate and one great dessert — that's your new system.
